verses.commentary: Faith is confidence in what we hope for and assurance about what we do not see. Not blind optimism but grounded certainty. Faith operates in the realm of the unseen but is not irrational. It rests on God's promise, not visible evidence.
This is what the ancients were commended for. The Old Testament saints were approved by God because of faith. Their lives testify that faith pleases God across all ages.

By faith we understand that the universe was formed at God's command, so that what is seen was not made out of what was visible. Creation ex nihilo requires faith. We did not witness it, yet we believe God spoke and it existed. Visible reality came from invisible word.

By faith Abel brought God a better offering than Cain did. By faith he was commended as righteous when God spoke well of his offerings. And by faith Abel still speaks, even though he is dead. Genesis 4 in view. Abel's offering was accepted because it was given in faith. His faith continues to testify centuries later.

By faith Enoch was taken from this life so he did not experience death. He could not be found because God had taken him away. For before he was taken, he was commended as one who pleased God. Genesis 5:24. Enoch walked with God and was translated without dying. Faith pleases God.

And without faith it is impossible to please God, because anyone who comes to Him must believe that He exists and that He rewards those who earnestly seek Him. Faith is non-negotiable. Two elements: belief in God's existence and confidence in His reward. God is not satisfied with mere acknowledgment; He seeks those who pursue Him.

By faith Noah, when warned about things not yet seen, in holy fear built an ark to save his family. By his faith he condemned the world and became heir of the righteousness that is in keeping with faith. Noah believed God's warning about the unseen flood. His obedience condemned the world's unbelief. Faith leads to action.

By faith Abraham, when called to go to a place he would later receive as his inheritance, obeyed and went, even though he did not know where he was going. Faith obeys without full knowledge. Abraham left everything familiar, trusting God's guidance. Obedience precedes understanding.

By faith he made his home in the promised land like a stranger in a foreign country; he lived in tents, as did Isaac and Jacob, who were heirs with him of the same promise. Abraham never possessed Canaan in his lifetime, yet he lived as a sojourner, trusting God's promise. Faith endures unfulfilled promises.

For he was looking forward to the city with foundations, whose architect and builder is God. Abraham's true hope was not earthly Canaan but the heavenly city. Faith sees beyond earthly promises to eternal realities. God Himself is the architect and builder.

And by faith even Sarah, who was past childbearing age, was enabled to bear children because she considered Him faithful who had made the promise. Sarah initially doubted but ultimately believed. Faith trusts God's faithfulness even when circumstances seem impossible. Her womb was dead, but God's promise was alive.

And so from this one man, and he as good as dead, came descendants as numerous as the stars in the sky and as countless as the sand on the seashore. From Abraham's dead body came innumerable descendants. Faith believes God can bring life from death, abundance from barrenness.

All these people were still living by faith when they died. They did not receive the things promised; they only saw them and welcomed them from a distance, admitting they were foreigners and strangers on earth. The patriarchs died without receiving fulfillment of God's promises. Yet they believed. Faith perseveres even without seeing completion.

People who say such things show they are looking for a country of their own. Their confession of being strangers reveals their longing for a homeland. Faith creates dissatisfaction with earthly things and longing for heavenly realities.

If they had been thinking of the country they had left, they would have had opportunity to return. They did not return to Mesopotamia. Their eyes were fixed forward, not backward. Faith does not retreat.

Instead, they were longing for a better country—a heavenly one. Therefore God is not ashamed to be called their God, for He has prepared a city for them. Their faith honored God; God honored them by identifying with them. He prepared an eternal city for those who sought one.

By faith Abraham, when God tested him, offered Isaac as a sacrifice. He who had embraced the promises was about to sacrifice his one and only son. Genesis 22. The supreme test: sacrifice the son of promise. Faith obeys even when obedience seems to contradict God's promise.

Even though God had said to him, It is through Isaac that your offspring will be reckoned. The promise depended on Isaac. Yet Abraham was willing to sacrifice him. Faith trusts God to fulfill His promises even when circumstances seem impossible.

Abraham reasoned that God could even raise the dead, and so in a manner of speaking he did receive Isaac back from death. Faith reasons from God's character. If God promised descendants through Isaac, and Isaac must die, then God will raise him. Abraham trusted resurrection before resurrection was common knowledge.
By faith Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau in regard to their future. Isaac's blessings were prophetic, spoken in faith that God would fulfill them. Faith speaks into the future based on God's promises.

By faith Jacob, when he was dying, blessed each of Joseph's sons, and worshiped as he leaned on the top of his staff. Jacob blessed his grandsons prophetically. Even in weakness (leaning on his staff), faith worships and blesses.

By faith Joseph, when his end was near, spoke about the exodus of the Israelites from Egypt and gave instructions concerning the burial of his bones. Joseph believed God's promise to bring Israel out of Egypt. He made arrangements for his bones to be carried out, a tangible expression of faith in future deliverance.

By faith Moses' parents hid him for three months after he was born, because they saw he was no ordinary child, and they were not afraid of the king's edict. Faith risks disobedience to earthly authority to obey God. They saw something special in Moses and defied Pharaoh's decree.

By faith Moses, when he had grown up, refused to be known as the son of Pharaoh's daughter. Faith chooses God's people over worldly privilege. Moses rejected Egyptian royalty to identify with enslaved Israel.

He chose to be mistreated along with the people of God rather than to enjoy the fleeting pleasures of sin. Faith calculates long-term over short-term. Suffering with God's people is better than temporary pleasures. Sin's pleasures are real but fleeting.

He regarded disgrace for the sake of Christ as of greater value than the treasures of Egypt, because he was looking ahead to his reward. Moses identified the Israelites' suffering with Christ's (typologically). Faith values eternal reward over earthly treasure. He looked beyond Egypt to God's reward.

By faith he left Egypt, not fearing the king's anger; he persevered because he saw Him who is invisible. Moses forsook Egypt trusting God, not fearing Pharaoh. Faith sees the invisible God more clearly than visible threats. Perseverance comes from fixing eyes on God.

By faith he kept the Passover and the application of blood, so that the destroyer of the firstborn would not touch the firstborn of Israel. Moses believed God's word about the plague and applied blood to doorposts. Faith obeys God's specific instructions for deliverance.

By faith the people passed through the Red Sea as on dry land; but when the Egyptians tried to do so, they were drowned. Faith walks on impossible paths God opens. Unbelief perishes attempting the same path. The difference: faith trusts God; presumption does not.

By faith the walls of Jericho fell, after the army had marched around them for seven days. Jericho's walls fell by faith, not military strategy. God's methods often seem foolish, but faith obeys and sees victory.

By faith the prostitute Rahab, because she welcomed the spies, was not killed with those who were disobedient. Rahab, a Gentile prostitute, believed Israel's God and was saved. Faith transcends ethnicity and past sin. She chose faith over her people's unbelief.

And what more shall I say? I do not have time to tell about Gideon, Barak, Samson, Jephthah, David, Samuel, and the prophets. The list is abbreviated. Countless others lived by faith. The catalog is representative, not exhaustive.

Who through faith conquered kingdoms, administered justice, gained what was promised; who shut the mouths of lions. Faith achieves mighty exploits: military victory, righteous governance, fulfilled promises, miraculous deliverance (Daniel).

Quenched the fury of the flames, and escaped the edge of the sword; whose weakness was turned to strength; and who became powerful in battle and routed foreign armies. Shadrach, Meshach, Abednego in the furnace. David escaping Saul. Weakness transformed to strength. Faith accomplishes the impossible.

Women received back their dead, raised to life again. There were others who were tortured, refusing to be released so that they might gain an even better resurrection. Elijah and Elisha raised the dead (1 Kings 17; 2 Kings 4). But some suffered torture, refusing deliverance, seeking eternal resurrection. Faith sometimes brings miraculous deliverance; sometimes costly endurance.

Some faced jeers and flogging, and even chains and imprisonment. Faith does not exempt from suffering. Jeremiah experienced this. Prophets were mocked, beaten, imprisoned. Faithfulness does not guarantee ease.

They were put to death by stoning; they were sawed in two; they were killed by the sword. They went about in sheepskins and goatskins, destitute, persecuted and mistreated. Stoning (Zechariah son of Jehoiada). Tradition says Isaiah was sawn in two. Elijah and Elisha wore rough garments. Faith sometimes leads to martyrdom and destitution.

The world was not worthy of them. They wandered in deserts and mountains, living in caves and holes in the ground. The world rejected them, but they were too good for the world. God's assessment reverses the world's. The faithful are the true nobility.

These were all commended for their faith, yet none of them received what had been promised. They died without seeing fulfillment. Faith persevered despite unfulfilled promises. Commendation came from God, not from receiving earthly fulfillment.

Since God had planned something better for us so that only together with us would they be made perfect. Old Testament saints await consummation with New Testament believers. Perfection is corporate, not individual only. We are all part of one body awaiting resurrection and glorification together.
